You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — reharmonization-techniques

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc and Pedagogical Structure

This book is a systematic course in jazz and contemporary reharmonization, organized as a cumulative skill progression that moves from the simplest harmonic substitutions to multi-layered combinatorial techniques. The pedagogy is strictly bottom-up: Chapters 1–4 establish the core vocabulary of diatonic function, tritone motion, and cadential engineering; Chapters 5–9 introduce borrowed and chromatic resources (modal interchange, bass-line construction, chord symbol correction, turnarounds, and modulation); Chapters 10–12 pivot to voicing mechanics and special chord types; and Chapters 13–16 synthesize everything into advanced modal, hybrid, and constant-structure frameworks. Throughout, the guiding ethic is restraint—substitution is selective, not wholesale, and each technique is accompanied by explicit stop-rules about when not to apply it.

## Foundational Harmonic Framework

Chapter 1 establishes the load-bearing taxonomy on which every subsequent technique depends. The seven diatonic chords are sorted into three functional families: the **tonic family** (I, III, VI), stable because it lacks the fourth scale degree; the **subdominant family** (II, IV, V7sus4), forward-moving because it contains the restless fourth; and the **dominant family** (V7, VII-7(b5), and contextually V7sus4), which drives resolution via the tritone between scale degrees 4 and 7. All substitution logic flows from these acoustic properties. Two hard voice-leading constraints are stated here and enforced throughout the book: avoid **b9 melody/harmony intervals** except in the V7(b9) resolution context, and avoid **tritone clashes on minor seventh chords** (with a specific II-7 exception in II-V cadences). The concept of **harmonic rhythm**—the density of chord changes per bar—is introduced as a control variable that governs phrase energy; increasing it accelerates tension, thinning it releases it.

## Cadential Engineering and Tritone Resources

Chapters 2–4 teach three progressively more chromatic methods for constructing cadences. Chapter 2 introduces the **diatonic approach**: working backward from a target chord, choosing approach chords with few common tones (typically two or fewer) via root movements that avoid thirds and sixths. Melody leads in approach chords must be restricted to chord tones (1, 3, 5, 7)—never extensions—to prevent b9 or tritone dissonances. The one sanctioned nondiatonic resource here is bVIIMaj7, borrowed from the parallel Dorian mode. Chapter 3 extends this to **tritone substitution** (dominant sevenths a tritone apart share the same tritone and can substitute for each other) and to **extended dominant chains** and **extended II-V7 patterns**, which build cascading forward motion toward a target. Altered tensions (b9, #9, b5, b13) typically resolve downward; interpolated minor sevenths can be strategically skipped; **displacement** moves target chords later in the phrase to create harmonic space. Chapter 4 deepens displacement, establishing two specific transformation rules: a b3 in the melody displaced by a half-step II-V7 pattern becomes tension 9 on the II-7, and a b5 in the lead of a minor 7(b5) chord becomes tension 11 on the II-7. Tritone substitution serves as the primary repair tool for melody-harmony clashes that displacement creates.

## Borrowed Harmony, Bass Lines, and Notation

Chapter 5 introduces **modal interchange (MI)**: chords drawn from parallel scales (natural minor, harmonic minor, melodic minor, Dorian) substitute for diatonic chords, with melody compatibility as the governing constraint. Unlike simple substitution, MI substitutions do not need to preserve functional category—they trade function for color. The key restriction is that minor-quality MI chords clash with melodies containing the major third (b9 interval), and the book prescribes a three-chord limit before returning to diatonic harmony to avoid tonal ambiguity. Chapter 6 reframes reharmonization as a **bass-line design problem**: a rough-draft stepwise bass line is iteratively refined, with bass notes treated as chord tones (not extensions), stepwise motion maintained, and consecutive dissonant intervals between melody and bass limited to three or fewer. Chapter 7 addresses the notational layer, establishing the rule that diatonic tensions (9ths, 13ths) are stylistically implied and should be omitted from chord symbols, while nondiatonic tensions must always be written explicitly. Seventh chords replace bare triads as the baseline vocabulary; enharmonic spellings are resolved by voice-leading context; line clichés are notated as a single chord with a moving line rather than a series of chromatic diminished sevenths.

## Form-Level Devices: Turnarounds, Endings, and Modulation

Chapters 8–9 lift the techniques to the phrase and song level. **Turnarounds** (Chapter 8) replace the long tonic chord at measures 31–32, following a four-slot template: tonic-function chord → link chord → subdominant chord → dominant chord resolving into the next phrase. The first chord can be drawn from tonic substitutions (III, VI), modal interchange chords, IV variants, or chromatic alterations; a compositional technique specific to turnarounds is shortening melody notes after two beats to free harmonic space for substitutions that would otherwise clash. Chapter 9 prescribes **extended endings** that resolve to a final major-seventh chord in a new key via deceptive cadence, using motivic melodic material from the song. **Modulatory interludes** are 2–8 bars long, treat the new-key downbeat as a target chord approached by strong root motion, and may use pickup-aware deceptive-cadence chords (VI-7(b5), bIIIMaj7). The III/VI tonic-substitution principle from Chapter 1 is explicitly cited as a construction tool here.

## Voicing Mechanics and Special Chord Types

Chapter 10 codifies **piano voice leading** as a set of motion rules keyed to root interval: for perfect-fourth or perfect-fifth root motion, resolve the third of the first chord to the seventh of the second (and vice versa); for unison or second motion, move all voices in parallel; for third or sixth motion, use either parallel or contrary motion. Tensions stay above the F on the fourth line of the bass clef. Inverted chords function as passing chords when approached and left by step; third-inversion chords require preceding root-position context. Chapter 11 covers **line clichés**—chromatic stepwise motion (typically half-steps) through the fifth and root of a sustained chord to add forward motion without changing harmony—most commonly applied to the tonic chord, with overuse on non-tonic chords warned against as tonally destabilizing. Chapter 12 defines **diminished seventh reharmonization** via a three-tiered hierarchy: substitute the dominant seventh that shares the tritone with the original diminished seventh; if there is no obvious dominant cadence to target, choose by root motion shape (ascending half-step favors dominants a perfect fifth or minor second above the target); if neither criterion applies, choose the dominant sharing the most chord tones with the original. The **chromatic II-V7 pattern**—a minor seventh followed by a dominant seventh a half step below—is defined here.

## Advanced Synthesis: Modal, Hybrid, and Constant-Structure Frameworks

The final three chapters present the book's most abstract resources. Chapter 13 teaches **modal reharmonization** across Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, and Aeolian, with rules requiring that both melody and chords be diatonic to the chosen modal scale, that the I chord occupy strong rhythmic positions approached by mode-specific two-chord cadences, and that each modal key be sustained for at least four bars so listeners register the color. Modal interchange—combining minor and major chords on the same pitch center—is distinguished from full modal reharmonization. The Lydian #11 requires a tonic pedal to be heard correctly; Phrygian b9 and Lydian tritone outside their modal context signal structural error. Chapter 14 introduces **hybrid chord voicings**: a triad or seventh chord (never augmented, diminished, or minor 7(b5)) layered above a fixed bass root, producing ambiguous altered-dominant texture derived theoretically from the altered dominant scale (1, b9, #9, 3, #4/b5, b6, b7, 1). The technique fails if the melody contains a major or minor third above the bass; hybrid voicings function best at phrase endpoints rather than throughout a passage. Chapter 15 defines **constant structure patterns**, which replace tonal function with textural consistency: bass motion follows a systematic interval pattern (half steps, whole steps, or thirds), tension tones appear commonly in the lead voice, and the roots may outline implied harmonies (diminished or major triads, tensions within a larger chord) without establishing functional tonality. Chapter 16 integrates all three: modal progressions reharmonized as hybrid chords, modal root motion driving constant structures, and pedal tones or ostinatos grounding ambiguous harmonies—with the explicit prescription that modal phrases and constant-structure patterns require **repetition** to ensure listener comprehension, often reinforced by tonic pedals that underscore modal quality.

# Chapter 1 — Simple Substitution

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

This chapter teaches the foundational harmonic framework for simple substitution reharmonization, organizing the seven diatonic chords into three functional families: the tonic family (I, III, VI) which sounds stable and rests at phrase endings, the subdominant family (II, IV, and V7sus4) which contains the restless fourth degree and moves forward moderately, and the dominant family (V7, VII-7(b5), and contextually V7sus4) which contains the tritone interval and drives strongly toward resolution. The master's characteristic move is anchoring all substitution rules to acoustic principles—the absence of the fourth degree explains tonic stability, the tritone explains dominant motion, and the b9 interval's dissonance explains why certain substitutions fail despite correct family membership. The chapter introduces the load-bearing terms chord function (a chord's tendency to move or remain stable), tritone (the augmented fourth between scale degrees 4 and 7 that creates forward motion), harmonic rhythm (the density of chords per bar, controlling phrase energy), and establishes two primary voice-leading constraints: avoid b9 melody/harmony intervals except in the V7(b9) resolution context, and avoid tritone clashes on minor seventh chords, with a specific II-7 exception in the II-V cadence. The procedural ethic—keep it simple, not all chords need changing—frames substitution as selective rather than wholesale.

# Chapter 2 — Diatonic Approach

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 introduces the diatonic approach technique, which constructs chord progressions by working backwards from a target chord—a harmonic destination that typically falls on the last or penultimate chord of a phrase. The foundational principle is that strong cadences occur when the approach chord and target chord share few common tones (typically two or fewer), achieved by choosing root movements that avoid thirds and sixths. To maintain a diatonic texture, the melody lead of approach chords must be restricted to chord tones (1, 3, 5, or 7), ensuring a homogenous, smooth sound without chromatic intrusions; extensions like 9, 11, or 13 risk unwanted dissonances like b9 or tritone intervals. The technique distinguishes three cadence types: strong (few common tones), weak or gentle (three or more common tones), and deceptive (V7 resolving to anything other than I). A practical safeguard against hyperactive harmonic rhythm is to limit the technique to short runs and to harmonize only melodic notes on rhythmic downbeats, while the one permitted nondiatonic chord—bVIIMaj7—adds richness by borrowing from the parallel Dorian mode.

# Chapter 3 — Tritone Substitution, Extended Dominant Seventh Chords, and Extended II-V7 Patterns

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches three interconnected reharmonization techniques for jazz standards: tritone substitution works backward from a target chord using dominant sevenths a perfect fifth apart, while altered tensions (b9, #9, b5, b13) typically resolve downward but permit upward motion in context. Extended dominant chains create forward harmonic momentum toward a target but risk rhythmic clutter, mitigated by placing chords only on strong beats and converting some dominants to minor sevenths for smoother texture. Extended II-V7 patterns combine these moves and work best with sparse melodic rhythms (quarters, dotted quarters, or halves) where one or two notes per chord allow quick resolution of clashes. Interpolated chords—typically minor sevenths between dominants—can be skipped strategically, and displacement (moving a target chord later in the phrase) stretches the progressions shape while creating harmonic space for new changes. Taken together, these techniques enable reharmonization that maintains forward motion and timbral sophistication without overwhelming the melody or tempo.

# Chapter 4 — Displacement

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 introduces displacement as a reharmonization technique that relocates dominant chords to create harmonic variation while maintaining melodic integrity. The core methods include pairing displaced dominants with II-V patterns (especially half-step higher), using tritone substitution to resolve melody-harmony clashes, and applying sus4 chord qualities when the melody contains tension notes that would clash with the new harmony. Two special cases establish transformational rules: when a b3 in the melody is displaced by a half-step II-V7 pattern, it becomes tension 9 on the II-7, and when b5 appears in the lead of a minor 7(b5) chord, displacement converts it to tension 11 on the II-7. The technique works most effectively with cadential dominants as targets, since these chords anchor the phrase structure and their displacement creates a noticeable shift in harmonic shape. Tritone substitution serves as a primary tool for fixing melody-harmony problems that arise from displacement, allowing the arranger to recontextualize tension notes as chord extensions rather than clashing elements.

# Chapter 7 — Correction of Faulty Chord Symbols

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 teaches the correction of faulty chord symbols in jazz standards through principles balancing precision and readability. The core asymmetry is that diatonic tensions (9ths and 13ths) should be omitted because they are stylistically implied, while nondiatonic tensions must always be included explicitly. Seventh chords form the baseline vocabulary of jazz chord notation and should replace bare triads to clarify harmonic function; for instance, IMaj7 and IVMaj7 are more analytically transparent than I and IV. When enharmonic equivalence allows multiple spellings of the same pitch collection—particularly with diminished sevenths—context and harmonic function determine the correct symbol, chosen to make voice-leading and resolution patterns legible. Practical errors to avoid include mixing sharp- and flat-key symbols within a phrase, using add instead of tension notation, and writing overly complex slash chords that obscure functional relationships; instead, simplify to symbols like Cmaj7 or G7sus4 that reveal harmonic intent. Line clichés are best notated as a single chord with a moving line rather than as a series of chromatic diminished sevenths.

# Chapter 8 — Turnarounds

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Turnarounds are reharmonized chord progressions that typically replace the long tonic chord in measures 31-32 of jazz standards, functioning as bridges between phrases or into new keys. The standard four-chord template positions a tonic-function chord first, followed by a link chord that resolves to target chords, then a subdominant chord, and finally a dominant chord that resolves into the next phrase. Common first-chord options include tonic substitutions (III and VI roots), modal interchange chords, IV variations, chromatic alterations like #V-7(b5), and bII or bVII modal interchange chords, all rooted in tonic or near-tonic function. Arrangers and composers personalize turnarounds by reharmonizing existing progressions, and the template can be expanded with extra measures for longer song forms. A key compositional technique involves shortening melody notes after two beats to create harmonic freedom for chord substitutions that would otherwise clash with sustained melodic pitches. Similar turnaround construction methods can also be employed to modulate into new keys, extending the device flexibility beyond simple phrase turnarounds.

# Chapter 10 — Basic Piano Voicing Techniques

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

Voice leading connects notes smoothly from chord to chord, creating a blended, textural sound while making voicings more playable on keyboard. Modern jazz and pop composers prioritize balanced note spacing and smooth chord-to-chord connections over strict traditional rules. The primary voice-leading rule governs perfect fourth or perfect fifth root motion: resolve the third of the first chord to the seventh of the second, and vice versa, producing clear harmonic texture and minimizing finger movement. For unison or second root motion, move all voices in parallel motion by similar amounts. When roots move by third or sixth, employ either parallel or contrary motion between the bass and upper voices. Tensions should generally stay above the F on the fourth line of the bass clef to avoid muddiness, and inverted chords function best as passing chords when approached and left by step rather than beginning or ending harmonic phrases. Non-chord tones in the lowest voice produce hybrid voicings (see Chapter 14), and third inversion chords require preceding root-position context to avoid being heard as a different root.

# Chapter 11 — Line Clichés

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch11.md`._

Line clichés are techniques that sustain a particular harmonic chord while adding forward momentum to a progression, typically achieved through stepwise motion (usually half-steps, though whole-step motion is also possible) between the fifth and root of the embellished chord. They are most commonly applied to the tonic (I) chord, though they may be used on any diatonic chord in shorter forms; however, overuse on non-tonic chords risks obscuring the sense of key by causing listeners to hear the embellished chord as a new harmonic entity. The harmonic rhythm of a line cliché should align with the performance tempo, requiring sparser, extended rhythms for very fast tempos. While whole-step motion may be employed to prevent melody-harmony clashes, the technique traditionally relies on half-step increments. Line clichés extend beyond minor keys to reharmonize melodies in major keys, and related passing-sevenths techniques can serve similar functions by increasing movement within a chord progression, always with root-position chords preceding seventh-over-bass voicings.

# Chapter 12 — Diminished Seventh Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch12.md`._

Diminished seventh chords serve as versatile reharmonization tools because four dominant seventh chords, each containing one of the tritones found in the original diminished seventh, can substitute for them. These dominant chords are located either a major third below each chord tone or a minor second below, providing multiple equivalent derivation methods. The choice among these four options depends primarily on the melody-harmony relationship and root motion into the target chord: ascending half-step motion typically favors dominants a perfect fifth above or minor second above the target, while descending root motion requires different selections. When no obvious dominant cadence exists to the target chord, the fallback rule is to choose the dominant that shares the most chord tones with the original diminished seventh while remaining compatible with the melody. This three-tiered decision hierarchy—prioritizing shared tritones, then dominant cadences, then chord-tone relationships—makes diminished sevenths powerful reharmonization devices that can smoothly connect to various targets through chromatic voice leading. The chapter also defines the chromatic II-V7 pattern, in which a minor seventh chord is followed by a dominant seventh a half step below.

# Chapter 14 — Hybrid Chord Voicings

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch14.md`._

Hybrid chord voicings create an altered dominant sound by layering a triad or seventh chord above a fixed bass note, producing ambiguous textures useful for phrase endings and contrast. The construction follows two key rules: retain the root as the bass note, and choose an upper-layer structure that is a major or minor triad, or a major, minor, or dominant seventh chord—avoiding augmented, diminished, or minor 7(b5) structures. The technique fails if the melody contains a major or minor third above the bass, which destroys the characteristic ambiguous texture and creates undesirable harmonic errors. Hybrid voicings work most effectively at phrase endpoints rather than throughout an entire passage, serving as deliberate tonal tools. The upper layer may be voiced flexibly as either a triad or seventh chord, and a single phrase can mix both forms, offering composers adaptability within the hybrid framework. The altered dominant scale (1, b9, #9, 3, #4/b5, b6, b7, 1) provides the theoretical basis for why hybrid voicings produce their distinctive altered sound.

# Chapter 15 — Constant Structure Patterns

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch15.md`._

Constant structure patterns organize sound through consistent bass motion (half steps, whole steps, or thirds) and maintain textural coherence via melody-harmony relationships, which form the primary organizational elements of the technique. While patterned root motion typically enhances the sound—such as roots outlining implied harmonies like diminished or major triads, or spelling tensions within larger chord structures—it is not strictly required. The roots may outline complete chords with their extensions (FMaj7 with tensions) or only loosely suggest tonal centers (I, IV, V) without establishing functional tonality, and tension tones commonly appear in the lead voice across most chords. This approach prioritizes consistent textural sound and harmonic outlining over traditional tonal function, creating non-tonal or loosely tonal progressions organized by systematic voice-leading patterns rather than chord-to-chord resolution.

# Chapter 16 — Combining Modal Patterns, Hybrid Chord Voicings, and Constant Structures

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch16.md`._

Chapter 16 presents three interconnected techniques for advanced harmonic development: reharmonizing modal progressions as hybrid chords, using modal root motion to drive constant structure patterns, and anchoring ambiguous harmonies with pedal tones or ostinatos. Effective modal progressions require careful melody selection—the melody must be diatonic to the chosen key and ideally resolve to the modal root to reinforce tonality. Hybrid chords gain textural power when deployed in series rather than in isolation, creating an impressionistic effect that develops the progression beyond traditional voicings. Pedal tones and ostinatos serve a crucial grounding function, establishing tonal clarity even when the underlying harmonic movement is ambiguous. To ensure listener comprehension, modal phrases and constant structure patterns require repetition, often with reinforcement from tonic pedals or ostinatos that underscore the modal quality. This integration of modal patterns, hybrid voicings, and constant structures—balanced with judicious repetition and textural consistency—offers a comprehensive toolkit for contemporary harmonic reharmonization.

## Three harmonic family system

> In order to use simple substitution as a reharmonization technique, you must understand
> the division of the seven diatonic chords into three groups or families. Each of these
> chord families has a function. A chord’s function is its tendency fo move or remain
> stable within a musical phrase.

**Significance (p. 6).** This defines the foundational analytical framework—chord families and function—on which the entire chapter's substitution method depends.

## Tonic family definition

> The tonic family of chords has a resting function. Chords in this group tend to sound
> stable. They have little sense of forward motion and are almost always found at the
> phrase endings of popular and standard tunes. Diatonic chords built on the first, third,
> and sixth degrees of a scale are the members of this group.

**Significance (p. 6).** This defines the tonic family (IMaj7, III-7, VI-7) and explains why these chords are interchangeable as simple substitutions.

## Fourth degree as tendency tone

> Tonic chords share several common tones. The chords are considered restful because
> they do not contain.the fourth degree. of the scale, which-is-F-in.the.key_of.C.

**Significance (p. 6).** This gives the acoustic reason why tonic chords are stable, anchoring the harmonic family logic to a concrete scale-degree rule.

## Subdominant family definition

> Chords in the subdominant family have a moderate tendency to move ahead within
> the musical phrase. All chords in this family contain the restless fourth degree of the
> scale. Chords built on the second and fourth scale degrees make up this group. The
> V7sus4 is also included in this family, because it contains the fourth scale degree instead
> of the third. (Using a suspended fourth instead of a third eliminates the tritone that gives
> a dominant family chord its characteristic sound. The tritone function is described below.}

**Significance (p. 7).** This defines the subdominant family and explains the borderline case of V7sus4, which is essential for correct substitution decisions.

## Dominant family and tritone

> Chords in the dominant family sound unresolved and have a strong tendency toward
> Tesolution. They are said to have a “moving” function. Dominant chords almost always
> precede phrase endings in popular and standard tunes. The chords V7, ViI-7(b5), and
> V7sus4 are in this family. (The V7sus4 chord has a dominant function when it resolves
>
> directly to IMaj7, even though it lacks the tritone interval.)
>
> G7 B-7(b5} — G7sus4
> 4 g e
> Sx ae
> Ga) S
> ry)
> V7 VII-7(b5) V7sus4
>
> Fig. 1.4. Dominant family (D] chords in the key of C
>
> V7 and VII-7(b5) share many common tones. They also contain both the fourth and -
> seventh scale degrees. The intervallic distance between these two notes is called a
> tritone, also known as an augmented fourth. The tritone’s highly restless sound
> produces a strong sense of forward motion. The fritone formed by the third and seventh
> of a dominant chord creates the chord’s strong forward motion. Dominant family chords
> often resolve to a chord in the tonic family.

**Significance (p. 7).** This defines the dominant family, identifies the tritone as the acoustic engine of forward motion, and specifies the V7sus4's contextual dual membership—all critical rules for substitution.

## Avoiding unwanted b9 intervals

> Avoid unwanted }9 (or 52) melody/harmony intervals when using simple
> substitution. The )2 interval is a half step. It is also known as a 59, which is an octave
> plus a minor second. This melody/harmony interval creates a dissonance strong enough =
> to destroy the basic function of the chord. In general, avoid choosing a substitute chord
> that creates a b9 interval with any one of the melody notes.

**Significance (p. 10).** This is the primary voice-leading rule constraining simple substitution—substitutions that create b9 clashes must be rejected regardless of correct family membership.

## V7(b9) exception to b9 rule

> The V7(b9] is the only common exception to this rule. The V7(b9) has become an
> acceptable sound in many pop and jazz songs. For example, a C7b9 moving to FMaj7
> in the key of F major works because the b9 is combined with a tritone interval, Both the
> b9 and tritone intervals follow established melodic tendencies when they resolve to the
> Fmaj7. Many listeners perceive 69 combinations that do not follow such well-established
> paths of resolution as errors or wrong notes.

**Significance (p. 10).** This carves out the single permitted b9 exception and explains its acoustic justification, preventing misapplication of the general rule.

## Avoiding tritone on minor seventh chords

> Avoid unwanted tritone (#4/!11) melody/harmony intervals on minor
> seventh chords.

**Significance (p. 11).** This is the second core constraint rule: a tritone melody/harmony clash on a minor seventh chord destroys its subdominant or tonic function by making it sound like a dominant structure.

## #4 on IVMaj7 vs. II-7 contrast

> It is interesting that the IVMaj7 chord (FMaj7) can be used with #4 in the melody, while
> its simple substitution, II-7(D~7), does not work as effectively with the same melody/
> harmony combination—even though both chords share subdominant function.

**Significance (p. 11).** This is a nuanced rule showing that family membership does not guarantee interchangeability when a specific melody note is present—IVMaj7 and II-7 diverge on #4 tolerance.

## Avoid b13/13 in lead of II-7

> The use of 13 in the lead of minor seventh chords, which produces a tritone with the
> third, is even more awkward when the minor seventh is a Il-7 followed by V7. The
> musical flow of the cadence seems more satisfying when the tritone interval and its
> greater sense of motion are reserved for the G7. Avoid using a 13 or b13 in the lead
> of II-7 chords.

**Significance (p. 11).** This extends the tritone avoidance rule specifically to the II-V context, giving a voice-leading reason to keep harmonic tension reserved for the dominant chord.

## Harmonic rhythm definition and effect

> Once you have chosen your primary chord substitutions, you can add additional chords
> to help smooth out the progression. Adding chords increases the number of chords used
> in each bar. This is referred to as increasing the harmonic rhythm.
>
> In general, more active harmonic rhythms produce a more energetic musical phrase,
> while slower harmonic rhythms are more languid.

**Significance (p. 12).** This defines harmonic rhythm and establishes the expressive rule linking chord density to energy level, a key tool for stylistic shaping of a reharmonization.

## Keep it simple rule

> Another general rule fo follow when reharmonizing popular tunes: keep it simple.
>
> Once you have chosen your primary chord substitutions, you can add additional chords
> to help smooth out the progression.

**Significance (p. 12).** This is an explicit procedural rule prioritizing restraint as the default approach before adding density, framing harmonic rhythm increase as a secondary step.

## Note: not all chords need changing

> Note: It is not necessary to change all of the original chords to get an interesting
> reharmonization.

**Significance (p. 13).** This practical rule corrects a common over-correction impulse, instructing students that partial substitution is valid and often preferable.

## Target chord definition

> A harmonic destination is referred to
> as a target chord. Ii is a critical structural element in the musical phrase. While any
> chord in a progression may be thought of as a potential target chord, successful target
> chords are often the last or nextto-last chords of a phrase.

**Significance (p. 17).** Defines the foundational concept of 'target chord' that the entire diatonic approach technique depends on.

## Strong cadence rule

> Strong cadences are created whenever the approach chord (the chord immediately
> preceding the target] and the target chord share no more than two pitches. The general
> rule is: strong cadences = few common tones between the cadential chord and its target.

**Significance (p. 19).** Gives the defining rule for identifying and constructing strong cadences, the primary vehicle for diatonic approach technique.

## Common tones and root motion

> Diatonie chords whose roots are a third or a sixth apart have many common
> tones. Diatonic chords whose roots are not a third or a sixth apart have fewer common
> fones.

**Significance (p. 19).** States the root-motion principle that determines common-tone count and therefore cadence strength.

## Diatonic approach chord tone rule

> In order to retain a diatonic sound, the melodic lead of the approach chord must be a
> chord tone—1, 3, 5, or 7.

**Significance (p. 19).** This is the central constraint rule of diatonic approach technique governing which melody/harmony relationships are permissible.

## Weak cadence definition

> Weak cadences, also referred to as gentle cadences, are produced when the
> approach chord and the target chord have three or more notes in common.

**Significance (p. 20).** Defines weak cadences with both the common-tone criterion and the root-motion criterion, distinguishing them from strong cadences.

## Deceptive cadence definition

> Deceptive cadences are produced when a dominant seventh chord moves
> unexpectedly. For example, a V7 chord is expected to resolve by perfect fifth to |
> major. Motion from the V7 to any chord other than IMaj7 is “deceptive.”

**Significance (p. 20).** Defines the deceptive cadence type for reference in subsequent chapters, completing the three-cadence taxonomy.

## Working backwards method

> Working backwards from the cadential approach chord, | support each of
> the remaining melody notes with a chord, making sure each of the chords is diatonic
> to the key and that each chord has a root in the lead.

**Significance (p. 21).** Describes the characteristic compositional procedure of diatonic approach: harmonizing a melody by working backwards from the target chord.

## Avoiding hyperactive harmonic rhythm

> This example exposes a poiential weakness of this technique: an extremely active
> harmonic rhythm. Active harmonic rhythm can easily sound overdone or too busy. You
> can avoid a hyperactive harmonic rhythm by limiting yourself to short “runs” of notes
> approaching a target chord.

**Significance (p. 21).** States an important practical rule against overuse of the technique, directing students to limit harmonic rhythm density.

## bVIIMaj7 as nondiatonic substitute

> There is one nondiatonic chord that may be used with. diatonic approach technique, the
> LVIIMaj7. In the key of C, the bVIIMaj7 is BbMaj7. It produces a rich sound without over-
> powering other diatonic chords.
>
> Though the BbMaj7 chord is not diatonic to C major, it blends smoothly with the other
> diatonic choices. That is because the BbMaj7 is diatonic to the C Dorian scale, a key that
> is parallel to C major.

**Significance (p. 22).** Introduces the one permitted nondiatonic chord and provides its theoretical justification via the parallel minor/Dorian relationship.

## Diatonic texture definition

> Diatonic texture is derived from a single scale or key. By limiting the lead of a diatonic
> approach chord to 1, 3, 5, or 7, one can be sure that the texture of the phrase will
> remain diatonic. The sound is homogenous and smooth.

**Significance (p. 22).** Defines diatonic texture and restates the 1/3/5/7 lead rule as the mechanism for maintaining it.

## 9, 11, 13 in lead — risk rule

> The use of 9, 11, or 13 in the lead greatly increases the possibility that the progression
> will quickly move out of the original key. it also means that you will more frequently
> encounter unwanted melody/harmony relationships, such as dissonant b9 or #11
> intervals. Though the result may sound interesting, its chromatic sound may be too
> dissonant for use in a mainstream pop style. To maintain a truly diatonic texture, restrict
> your choice of melody/harmony combinations fo 1, 3, 5, or 7 in the lead of the
> diatonic chords.

**Significance (p. 23).** Gives an explicit rule against extended-interval leads (9/11/13) for maintaining diatonic texture in mainstream pop style.

## Avoid b9 and tritone combinations

> No matter which technique you use, always avoid poor
> melody/harmony combinations—unwanted b9s and tritones.

**Significance (p. 23).** States the universal melody/harmony rule that applies across all reharmonization techniques in the book.

## Harmonizing downbeats only

> To reharmonize, diatonic chords (and a bVIIMaj7) with 3 in the lead support melody
> notes that fall on rhythmic downbeats. | do not harmonize melodic notes that fall on
> weaker rhythmic beats.

**Significance (p. 24).** Provides a practical technique for applying diatonic approach to non-quarter-note melodies without creating overly dense harmonic rhythms.

## Tritone substitution procedure

> To use tritone substitution, start with a target chord. Then, work backward from the target
> chord, harmonizing each melody note with a series of dominant seventh chords a perfect
> fifth apart.

**Significance (p. 28).** States the core procedural rule for tritone substitution — direction (backward), interval (perfect fifth), and starting point (target chord).

## Altered tension resolution tendency

> Chord tensions are called altered when they are a half step above or below a 5,
> major 9, or a major 13. In the performance practice of pop and jazz standard tunes,
> altered tensions such as the 69, #9, b5, b13 melody notes have most often been resolved
> in a downward direction.

**Significance (p. 29).** Defines 'altered tension' and establishes the core performance-practice rule that these tones resolve downward — underpins Errors 2, 4, and 5 in the error table.

## Exception rule for upward movement of altered tensions

> Almost any motion up or down from b9, #9, b5, or b13 is possible.

**Significance (p. 30).** Qualifies the downward-resolution rule: upward motion is allowed but must be contextually justified, not reflexive — a key nuance for application.

## Extended dominants: purpose and overuse warning

> The strong sense of forward motion and resolution created by reharmonizing with chains
> of extended dominants helps drive short runs of notes toward a target chord. As with
> diatonic approach technique, be careful not to overuse this method, since it can produce
> a very busy harmonic rhythm that overpowers the melody.

**Significance (p. 30).** Names the harmonic function of extended dominant chains (forward motion toward target) and provides a practical constraint against overuse.

## Definition of interpolated chord

> Any chord placed between two dominant chords may be described as interpolated.
> This new chord briefly delays resolution. Commonly, the interpolated chord is a minor
> seventh chord placed between two dominants in a pattern.

**Significance (p. 31).** Defines 'interpolated chord' — a structural concept used throughout subsequent examples and reharmonization decisions.

## Skipping interpolated chords in extended dominant chains

> Sometimes, you may choose to ignore a single interpolated or interrupting chord when
> reharmonizing with extended dominant chords.

**Significance (p. 31).** Permits a specific reharmonization option (skipping interpolated chords), expanding the set of valid moves when applying the extended dominant technique.

## Reducing chord density at fast tempos

> Chords appear only on strong beats, 1 and 3, of the second measure
> in order fo maintain the extended dominant sound while reducing the number of chords
> per bar. Reducing the number of chords in each bar will help to produce a smoother
> sound, and is particularly desirable if the performance tempo is medium to fast.

**Significance (p. 32).** Teaches a practical density-management rule: limiting chords to strong beats preserves the extended-dominant effect while avoiding rhythmic clutter at faster tempos.

## Converting dominant chords to minor sevenths for smoother texture

> More variations of these patterns may be found by converting dominant chords that fall
> on strong beats to minor seventh chords. This yields a slightly smoother texture.

**Significance (p. 33).** States a transformation rule (dominant → minor seventh on strong beats) that produces a distinct timbral outcome — foundational to extended II-V7 patterns.

## Rhythmic suitability for extended dominant / II-V7 technique

> Notice that these melodies consist mainly of quarter notes, dotted quarter notes, or half
> notes. The extended dominant or extended IL-V7 technique works best with melodic
> rhythms of this sort. With only one or two melody notes per chord, you can quickly solve
> melody/harmony clashes with tritone substitutions.

**Significance (p. 35).** Specifies the rhythmic conditions under which the entire chapter's technique is most effective — a scope limitation the student must understand before applying the method.

## Definition and effect of displacement

> Displacement changes the shape of a harmonic progression by moving a target chord
> to a point later in the progression. This chord movement maintains emphasis on the target
> chord, but stretches the overall shape of the progression by shifting the locations of har-
> monic cadences.
>
> Displacing a target chord creates an open space within the phrase. That open space can
> then be filled with new chord changes. If a target chord is displaced by only a beat or
> two, the reharmonization will be similar to the original phrase. Displacement of more
> than a measure will create a more dramatic change.

**Significance (p. 36).** Defines displacement as a reharmonization technique, explains its mechanical effect (open space), and calibrates the relationship between displacement amount and degree of harmonic change.

# Chapter 6 — Reharmonization Using Bass Lines

_Pages 53-64._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Bass line direction and cadential strength

> Bass lines usually move toward target chords with strong, stepwise root motion, Chords
> built upon these notes will have a strong cadential flow, and will sound most effective at
> phrase endings.

**Significance (p. 53).** Foundational thesis: bass-line root motion drives cadence.

## Consistency of bass-line direction

> Be sure to move your bass lines consistently up or down into the target chord. Failure to
> maintain a consistent direction will weaken the cadential possibilities of the chord
> progressions derived from the bass line.

**Significance (p. 54).** Prescriptive rule with explicit consequence.

## Dissonance limit between melody and bass

> Also, consider the interval combinations between the melody and the bass line. If you
> desire a smooth reharmonization, avoid more than three consecutive dissonant intervals
> between the bass line and the melody. (In this context, dissonant intervals are seconds,
> fourths, fifths, and sevenths. Minor sevenths, major seconds, and augmented
> fourths/diminished fifths are the most dissonant combinations.)

**Significance (p. 54).** Specific quantified constraint on dissonance.

## Treating bass notes as chord tones 1 3 5 7

> Think of the bass notes as chord tones 1, 3, 5, or 7 of potential reharmonized chord
> changes. Thinking of these notes as 9, 11, or 13 will produce hybrid chord voicings
> (see chapter 14) or may produce chord symbols that are overly complex (see chapter 7).

**Significance (p. 54).** Core practical rule for deriving chords from bass notes.

## Third-inversion chords: prerequisite root-position context

> Be cautious about using chord tone 7 in the bass (third inversion), unless a root position
> form of the same chord is used directly before the inversion.

**Significance (p. 55).** Voice-leading prerequisite for third-inversion chords.

## Hybrid chords: ambiguity

> if the chords are not in this sequence, the chord structure with the 7 in the bass will often
> sound like an incomplete voicing of the bass note. For example: C7 with a Bb in the bass
> (symbol: C/B) will be heard as a type of Bb chord with tensions 9, #11, and 13, unless
> it has a C7 before it to establish context.

**Significance (p. 56).** Explains the perceptual mechanism of hybrid chord ambiguity.

## Hybrid chords: definition and warning

> These incomplete, ambiguous voicings are called hybrid chords. They have a
> distinctive sound and texture, and should be used carefully. If used indiscriminately,
> hybrids can break up the sonic texture and mood of a phrase.

**Significance (p. 56).** Defines hybrid chords and articulates the aesthetic risk.

## Harmonic rhythm: keep it simple

> Remember the rule: Keep it simple. When working with busy harmonic rhythms in which
> there are two, three, or four chords per measure, relatively simple tonal progressions
> work best.

**Significance (p. 56).** Named rule constraining chord choice under dense harmonic rhythm.

## Step-by-step method for bass-line reharmonization

> Think of the first bass line as a rough draft, and subsequent versions as continuing drafts
> on the way to the final reharmonization of the phrase. Here is a general step-by-step
> method for bass-line reharmonization.
>
> ]. Choose a target chord.
>
> 2. Create a rough draft bass line. Start with stepwise descending half notes into
> the target chord.

**Significance (p. 58).** Introduces the iterative draft-based methodology.

## Adding chromatic variations to bass line

> 5. Experiment with your bass line by adding chromatic varidtions to it.

**Significance (p. 59).** Step 5 of the method.

## Melodic variation as a reharmonization tool

> A few subtle changes to the melody line may be used to solve melody/harmony conflicts
> and to provide color and variety to the reharmonization. This approach works best with
> melodies that are extremely familiar. It should not be overused.

**Significance (p. 61).** Introduces melodic alteration as an additional degree of freedom.

## Exercise guidance: variety in bass-line rhythm

> Melody and bass line combinations can yield a huge variety of reharmonization
> choices. In these exercises, experiment with a variety of bass line rhythms. For example,
> try one example with mostly quarter notes in the bass, the next with half notes, and the
> last with a mixture of bass note rhythms. Supply a Roman numeral analysis with each
> reharmonization.

**Significance (p. 62).** Mandates Roman numeral analysis in exercises.

# Chapter 7 — Correction of Faulty Chord Symbols

_Pages 65-72._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. ## Diatonic tensions implied in jazz style

> While diatonic tensions are implied in standard jazz style, all nondiatonic tensions
> should be included in the chord symbols.

**Significance (p. 67).** Core rule: asymmetry between diatonic (omit) and nondiatonic (always include) tensions.

## Triadic chord symbols inappropriate in jazz standards

> In Fig. 7.3, the use of triads is inappropriate and stylistically incorrect. The standard tune
> style is not presenied clearly by triadic chord symbols. Standard jazz chord symbols
> usually include additional pitches—such as sevenths.

**Significance (p. 65).** Why seventh chords are baseline vocabulary.

## Effect of seventh chords on harmonic analysis clarity

> Changing the chord symbols to seventh chords brings their representation into sync with
> the normal chord vocabulary of jazz standards. The use of major seventh chord symbols
> on Eb and Ab makes the chords easier to analyze as IMaj7 and IVMaj7.

**Significance (p. 66).** Functional payoff: harmonic function becomes legible.

## Diatonic tensions are overkill to write explicitly

> Tensions 9 and 13 are diatonic to the key and are
> automatically implied in a standard tune style. Because diatonic tensions are part of jazz
> style, it would be considered overkill to write them out explicitly. Often, when a writer
> includes diatonic tensions in the chord symbol, he or she really is suggesting a specific
> chord voicing without writing it out in notation.

**Significance (p. 67).** Reasoning behind the omit-diatonic-tensions rule.

## Avoid mixing sharp- and flat-key chord symbols

> Another common chord symbol notation error is to mix chord symbols derived from sharp
> keys and flat keys within the same short phrase. It is visually confusing, and will make it
> much harder for the musicians to read and perform the music correctly.

**Significance (p. 68).** Names error and practical consequence for performers.

## Avoid the word 'add' in chord symbols

> Avoid the word “add” in chord symbols. Tension 11 is mislabeled this way most offen.

**Significance (p. 68).** Direct prescriptive rule with most frequent offender identified.

## Diminished seventh chords: context determines correct symbol

> Any diminished seventh can be
> expressed as a series of four enharmonically equal chord symbols. The chords below
> share the same chord tones. However, context will determine which chord symbol best
> represents the sound and harmonic function of the chord.

**Significance (p. 68).** Enharmonic equivalence means symbol choice is a functional decision.

## C#°7 preferred over E°7 to show dominant function

> C#°7, on the other hand, contains all the tones of A7, the dominant of D minor, without
> the root. Using C#°7 to represent these pitches makes the dominant resolution between
> these two chords much easier to grasp.

**Significance (p. 69).** Concrete demonstration of enharmonic respelling clarifying function.

## Two clues a line cliché is hidden in a progression

> Two clues that a line
> cliché is hidden in the progression are:
>
> 1. Presence of a secondary dominant chord with an augmented quality
>
> 2. Presence of a diminished seventh that does not resolve by step

**Significance (p. 69).** Diagnostic criteria for hidden line clichés.

## Line cliché notation principle

> A line cliché is most clearly represented as a single chord with a line moving against it.
> Diminished seventh chords normally resolve by half-step root motion.

**Significance (p. 69).** Notational prescription for line clichés.

## Overly complex symbols hide tonic function

> This chord progression is overly complex and hides the tonic (T) function of the C triad.
> Also, G°7 has a dominant sound approaching F6, but the dominant relationship is not
> clearly expressed by the chord symbol.

**Significance (p. 70).** Dual problem: obscured tonic, unexpressed dominant relationship.

## Slash chords vs. functional symbols

> Complex chord symbols like E-7/C or D~7/G may sometimes be clear and
> functionally accurate if they show true hybrid chord function or if they are part of a
> progression over a sustained note—a pedal tone.

**Significance (p. 70).** Slash notation is correct when reflecting hybrid or pedal function.

## Simplifying slash chords to reveal harmonic function

> To simplify and clarify the chord symbols, use Cmaj7 and G7sus4. They represent the
> same vertical pitches as in the example above, but are expressed in a way that clearly
> reveals their harmonic function.

**Significance (p. 70).** Prescriptive fix: functional clarity is the criterion for a better symbol.

# Chapter 8 — Turnarounds

_Pages 73-85._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Definition and placement of turnarounds

> Turnarounds are reharmonized within the existing song form. The turnaround is often
>
> specifically designed to replace the long IMaj7 chord found in measures 31 and 32 of
> many jazz standards.

**Significance (p. 73).** Foundational definition.

## First chord of a turnaround

> The first chord of a turnaround (measure 31) can be any chord that supports the melodic
> line, but is often a chord with tonic function.

**Significance (p. 74).** Governing rule for first chord position.

## Second chord of a turnaround — the link

> The second chord of a typical turnaround
> (measure 31, beat 3) is a chord that connects forward to one of the chords in measure
> 32. Think of the chords in measure 32 as target chords. The second chord of the
> turnaround resolves by strong root motion into one of the targets. It forms a link between
> the first and third or fourth chords in the turnaround.

**Significance (p. 74).** Defines the link function and introduces target-chord concept.

## Third chord of a turnaround — subdominant

> The third chord of a typical turnaround (measure 32, beat 1} is a chord that has a
> subdominant function (SD) and leads by strong root motion into the last chord of the
> turnaround.

**Significance (p. 74).** Assigns subdominant function to third chord position.

## Fourth chord of a turnaround — dominant

> The fourth and usually last chord of a typical turnaround (measure 32, beat 3) is a chord
> that has dominant function and leads by strong root motion into the first chord of the new
>
> phrase.

**Significance (p. 74).** Completes the four-chord template specifying dominant function.

## Personal reharmonization of turnarounds

> An arranger or composer may rewrite an existing turnaround to give the tune a personal
> flavor.

**Significance (p. 73).** Frames reharmonization as creative tool.

## Common first-chord options

> most of the turnarounds here begin with a chord in the
>
> tonic family or a chromatic variation of a tonic family chord.

**Significance (p. 76).** Distills common-practice norm.

## Catalog of viable first-chord types

> ® tonic substitution (variations on the III-7 and VI-7)
>
> ® modal interchange chord with a root based on Ill or VI
> © IV chord or modal interchange variation of IV {IV-, IV-6, IV-7}
> © #V-7(b5) chord (a chord that is considered a chromatic variation of IV}
>
> © bIIMaj7 (a modal interchange chord that shares many common tones with IV-]
> e bVIIMaj7 or bVII7 (modal interchange chords that share two common tones
>
> with IV or IV-}

**Significance (p. 77).** Comprehensive enumeration of first-chord options.

## Tonic substitution between I and VI

> You may think
> of the “A” root of the chord as VI in the key. In this case, it functions as a kind of
> substitution for IMaj7, since | and VI both have tonic function within any key and may
>
> substitute for each other.

**Significance (p. 79).** Theoretical basis for VI-rooted turnaround openers.

## Tonic substitution between I and III

> Similarly, E7 (V/VI} could be used as the first chord of a turnaround. It is based on a Ill
>
> root. | and Ill both have fonic function within any key and may substitute for each other.

**Significance (p. 79).** Extends tonic substitution to III root.

## Turnarounds that lead to a new key

> A similar technique may be used fo construct turnarounds that lead to a new key.

**Significance (p. 82).** Opens modulating-turnaround section.

## Shortening the melody note for harmonic freedom

> As a general rule, you can drop
> the melody note after it has been established for at least two beats. This new open space
> in the melody allows you to insert other chord choices that would have clashed with the
> long Eb in the lead.

**Significance (p. 83).** Melody duration governs available substitutions.

## Turnaround with an extra measure

> The turnaround below contains an extra measure. This can be an effective choice in
> some standard tunes with longer forms.

**Significance (p. 77).** Four-chord template is expandable.

# Chapter 10 — Basic Piano Voicing Techniques

_Pages 95-104._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Voice leading definition and purpose

> Voice leading
> connects notes smoothly from chord to chord with a blended, textural sound. It also
> makes the voicings easier fo play on a keyboard.

**Significance (p. 96).** Core definition: states sonic goal and practical benefit.

## Jazz/pop voice leading guidelines

> Current jazz and pop composers are less concerned with traditional voice leading rules,
> but still strive for balanced note spacing and reasonably smooth connection from one
> chord to the next.

**Significance (p. 96).** Governing aesthetic premise of the chapter.

## Resolution of chord tones 3 and 7

> If the root motion moves by perfect fourth or perfect fifth, resolve chord tone 3
> in the first chord to chord tone 7 of the second, and chord tone 7 in the first
> chord to 3 in the second, as shown in the following examples. These
> resolutions produce a clear harmonic texture and minimize finger movement
> from chord to chord.

**Significance (p. 96).** Most important voice-leading rule.

## Parallel motion rule for unison/second

> If root motion moves by unison or second, move the voices in parallel motion.
> Parallel motion means that all voices move up or down by a similar amount.

**Significance (p. 97).** Second principal rule for unison/second case.

## Contrary and parallel motion for third/sixth

> If roots move by third or sixth, use either parallel or contrary motion between
> the roots and the upper voices. Contrary motion means that voices move in
> opposite directions: the bass note moves down, while all other voices move
>
> up.

**Significance (p. 97).** Completes three-case taxonomy.

## Tension placement register rule

> It is generally good practice to keep all tensions above
> the F found on the fourth line of the bass clef in order to avoid a muddy
>
> sound,

**Significance (p. 98).** Register constraint for all tension voicings.

## Non-chord tones in lowest voice create hybrid voicings

> Non-chord tones (tensions 9, 11, 13) found in the lowest voice produce a hybrid voicing.
>
> (See chapter 14.}

**Significance (p. 100).** Defines the boundary between inversion and hybrid voicing.

## Inversions as passing chords

> It is usually best to move away from an inverted chord by step. Inversions function as
> passing chords; they rarely begin or conclude harmonic phrases. The use of an inversion
> does not change the functional analysis of a progression in any way.

**Significance (p. 100).** Functional role of inversions: voice-leading/coloristic, not functional.

## Solution for third inversion ambiguity

> For a smooth, traditional sound, precede each third inversion chord with the same chord
> in root position.

**Significance (p. 101).** Prescriptive remedy: precede with root-position version.

## Definition and purpose of line cliché

> The line cliché allows the writer to sustain a particular chord while adding momentum to
> the progression.

**Significance (p. 109).** Core functional definition.

## Warning about overuse on non-tonic chords

> However, repeated use of a line cliché on a chord other than the “I”
> may cause the listener to hear the chord being embellished by the line cliché as a new |
> chord. Overuse of this technique may weaken the sense of key within the progression.

**Significance (p. 109).** Tonal-center risk constraint.

## Primary location of line clichés

> Although line clichés are found on the | chord more offen than on other diatonic chords,
> a short line cliché (one measure or less) may be applied to any diatonic chord.

**Significance (p. 109).** Normative home of the technique with broader licensing.

## Half-step vs whole-step motion

> Although most line clichés move in half-step increments, whole step motion between the
>
> fifth and the root is also possible.

**Significance (p. 106).** Defines intervallic constraint.

## Whole-step motion to avoid clashes

> The moving line illustrates that
> the descending {or ascending) line does not have to move exclusively by half steps.
> Whole-step motion also works, and is sometimes needed to prevent melody/harmony
> clashes.

**Significance (p. 107).** Functional reason for whole-step choice.

## Harmonic rhythm matched to tempo

> The harmonic rhythm should fit with the performance tempo.

**Significance (p. 108).** Governing principle for harmonic rhythm selection.

## Very fast tempo requires sparser rhythm

> If performance tempo is very fast, an even more sparse and extended harmonic rhythm
> would be a good choice.

**Significance (p. 108).** Extends tempo-rhythm relationship.

## Range of the chromatic line

> The line cliché movement is most often in half steps between the fifth and root of
>
> the chord being embellished.

**Significance (p. 106).** Structural pitch range definition.

## Line cliché in major keys

> Line clichés may also be used to reharmonize melodies in major keys.

**Significance (p. 109).** Extends technique beyond minor.

## Passing sevenths as related technique

> Short passing notes against a single chord may be used in the same spirit as a full line
> cliché. The reharmonized example below uses stepwise, passing sevenths in the bass fo
> increase the sense of movement within the chord progression.

**Significance (p. 110).** Introduces related passing-sevenths technique.

## Root position precedes seventh-over-bass voicing

> Notice that a
> chord in root position always precedes a chord voiced over its seventh.

**Significance (p. 110).** Voice-leading rule for passing-sevenths.

# Chapter 12 — Diminished Seventh Chords

_Pages 113-122._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Four dominant chords related to a diminished seventh

> Four dominant seventh chords are related to a given diminished seventh chord. These
> four dominant chords (with or without their related I|-7s) are the most common choices
> for reharmonizing a diminished seventh. Each of these dominant chords contains one of
> the tritones found in the original diminished seventh.

**Significance (p. 113).** Foundational premise of the chapter.

## Method 1: dominant chords a major third below

> To find the four dominant chords related to a given diminished seventh chord, look down
> a major third below each chord tone in the diminished seventh.

**Significance (p. 113).** Primary derivation method.

## Method 2: a minor second below

> Another way to find the chords related to a diminished seventh is to locate the note a
> minor second below each chord tone of the diminished chord.

**Significance (p. 115).** Equivalent alternative derivation.

## Practical determinant of substitute choice

> In practice, the substitute chord is determined by the melody/harmony relationship and
> by the root motion into the target chord.

**Significance (p. 116).** Two real-world selection criteria.

## Ascending half-step motion: preferred choices

> Diminished seventh chords that move up by half step to a chord in root position are most
> often reharmonized with dominant sevenths that are a perfect fifth abdve or a minor
> second above the target.

**Significance (p. 116).** Voice-leading rule for ascending half-step resolution.

## Descending root motion changes choice

> The following example shows another common diminished seventh pattern. The
> descending root motion changes the choice of reharmonization. Replacing the
> diminished seventh chord with the dominant or substitute dominant (subV} of the
> target chord does not fit as smoothly as in the ascending patterns.

**Significance (p. 117).** Marks the pivotal exception case for descending motion.

## Fallback rule: shared chord tones

> None of the four choices has an obvious dominant cadence leading into the D-7 target
> chord. That is, none of the dominant sevenths related to Eb°7 is V7 of D-7 or subV7 of
> D-7. Under these conditions, look for the chord that is most strongly related fo the chord
> structure of the original diminished seventh chord.

**Significance (p. 117).** Fallback decision rule when dominant cadence fails.

## Three-rule summary for diminished seventh reharmonization

> 1. When reharmonizing diminished seventh chords, choose a dominant
> chord that shares one of the tritone intervals found in the original
> diminished seventh.
>
> 2. When possible, also choose a chord that forms a dominant cadence into
> the target chord.
>
> 3. When a dominant cadence to the target chord is not available, choose a
> dominant chord that shares a considerable number of chord tones with the
> original diminished seventh. Be sure it is compatible with the melody.

**Significance (p. 119).** Explicit decision hierarchy.

## Definition of chromatic II-V7 pattern

> A chromatic II-V7 pattern is made up of a minor seventh chord
> or a minor 7(b5) followed by a dominant seventh a half step below. The root motion
> between the two chords is chromatic and the two chords sound subdominant fo dominant
> in the same spirit as regular II-V7 patterns.

**Significance (p. 119).** Defines chromatic II-V7 concept.

## Altered dominant sound

> Hybrid structures of the type shown above produce an altered dominant sound.

**Significance (p. 147).** Theoretical basis for hybrid voicings.

## Rules for constructing hybrid voicings

> To construct hybrid voicings of traditional chord structures:
>
> 1. Keep the root of the original chord.
>
> 2. Choose an upper structure layer that forms a major or minor triad or
> forms a major seventh, a minor seventh, or a dominant seventh. Avoid
> augmented, diminished, or minor 7(5) structures in the upper layer of hybrid
> chords since they tend to sound like more traditional chord voicings when
>
> combined with the bass note.

**Significance (p. 147).** Primary prescriptive rule set.

## When hybrids fail — third in lead

> Hybrid voicings lose their effectiveness if melody notes form intervals of either a major
> or minor third above the bass note. Errors of several kinds are produced if a third is in
> the lead:

**Significance (p. 149).** Primary constraint/failure condition.

## Error type 2 — loss of ambiguous texture

> 2. The third above the bass note will destroy the ambiguous texture associated
> with this hybrid technique.

**Significance (p. 149).** Second error; defines aesthetic property.

## Selective application of hybrid voicings

> A hybrid voicing often works well at the end of a phrase. The entire phrase need not be
> harmonized with hybrid chords.

**Significance (p. 149).** Hybrids are tools for contrast.

## Upper layer may be triad or seventh

> The upper layer of a hybrid chord may be voiced as either a triad or a seventh chord.

**Significance (p. 150).** Voicing flexibility.

## Mixing triads and seventh chords

> A given phrase may contain a mixture of both triads and seventh chords used in the
> upper layer of hybrid chord voicings.

**Significance (p. 150).** Permits mixing.

## Constant structure bass patterns

> Use of structured patterns in the bass (consistent motion up or down by half steps,
> whole steps, or major or minor thirds) helps to organize the sound of constant structure
> progressions.

**Significance (p. 156).** Names valid bass interval patterns.

## Primary organizational elements

> The consistent textural sound of
> constant structures and melody/harmony relationships form the primary organizational
>
> elements of this technique.

**Significance (p. 156).** Texture and M/H relationships define technique.

## Patterned root motion not required

> Patterned, consistent root motion usually has a positive effect on the sound
> of constant structure progressions, but is not required.

**Significance (p. 156).** Enhancement, not requirement.

## Root motion outlining diminished triad

> The roots of the constant structure major seventh chords in the example below outline an
> F# diminished triad (F#, A, C). This progression is organized by the root motion but is not
> clearly tonal.

**Significance (p. 157).** Roots can spell implied harmony non-tonally.

## Chord roots spelling major triad

> The roots of the constant structure chords in the example below outline a D triad:
>
> D, FR, A.

**Significance (p. 157).** Second application of root-spelling principle.

## Roots outlining FMaj7 with tensions

> The roots of the constant structure chords in fig. 15.

**Significance (p. 158).** Roots tracing complete chord with tensions.

## Melodic tensions as common

> The lead in most of the chords is a tension;

**Significance (p. 158).** Tension tones as stylistic norm.

## Loose tonal outline

> The constant structure dominant seventh sus4 chords in fig. 15.8 loosely outline I, IV, and
> V in the key of A, but are not clearly tonal.

**Significance (p. 157).** Loosely outlining vs functional tonality.

## Four reharmonization options

> Options:
> © Revoice the chords of the modal progression as hybrids.
> @ Use the root motion from the modal progression as the root motion of a
> constant structure pattern.
> e Use pedal tones or ostinato patterns to support any progression produced by
> these means.

**Significance (p. 167).** Practical decision tree.

## Melody selection criterion

> Choose a song fragment that contains a diatonic melody. The fragment below
> could be diatonic fo more than one key.

**Significance (p. 167).** Prerequisite condition.

## Pedal tone and tonality

> The use of an A pedal in the example below makes the phrase sound as if it is
> in the key of A, despite the ambiguous nature of thé constant structure progression.

**Significance (p. 165).** Pedal tones supply tonal grounding.

## Reinforcing modal quality

> If you seek a strong modal quality, repeat the | chord
> and its characteristic cadential chords offen to drive the point home. Use of a tonic pedal
> tone or tonic ostinato will help to reinforce the sense of-modal tonality.

**Significance (p. 169).** How to make modal progression convincing.

## Constant structure repetition requirement

> Modal phrases and constant structure examples normally require repetition io develop a
> clear meaning in the listener’s ear.

**Significance (p. 169).** Perceptual rule for repetition.

## Hybrid chords: isolated vs series

> Hybrid chords may occasionally be used as isolated structures within phrases, but they
> acquire a stronger textural personality when used in a series. A series of hybrid chords
> produces a stronger impressionistic effect than an isolated hybrid voicing.

**Significance (p. 169).** Two deployment strategies.

## Root motion as reharmonization basis

> Use the root motion from the modal progression as the root motion of a
> constant structure pattern.

**Significance (p. 167).** Bridges modal and constant structures.

## Criteria for modal progression choice

> Two other considerations were important in this choice:
> 1. All melody notes within this fragment are diatonic to A minor.
>
> 2. The melody ends on A in measure 3, which reinforces the modal character of
>
> the phrase.

**Significance (p. 164).** Selection criteria for modal root motion.

## Hybrid voicings develop the progression

> Voicing all or some of the chords in the modal progression as hybrid structures develops
> the chord progression further.

**Significance (p. 163).** Hybrid voicing as developmental tool.

## Variety with textural consistency

> This introduces some variety, yet maintains a similar chordal texture
> throughout the phrase.

**Significance (p. 166).** Aesthetic balance when combining techniques.
